periods.objective: Solve multi-step linear equations using the distributive property and variables on both sides.
periods.workedExamples: 9-1, Examples 1-3 — Solve 3(x + 4) = 2x + 15; Solve 5x − 3 = 2x + 9; Solve Equation with Fractions
periods.notes: Distributive property, variables on both sides, and clearing fractions by multiplying by the LCD.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9a with one retrieval prompt solving a two-step equation.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-1, Examples 1-3 to develop: solve equations using the distributive property to remove parentheses, combine like terms with variables on both sides, and clear fractions by multiplying every term by the LCD.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-1, Examples 1-3: solve multi-step equations with distribution, variable movement, and fraction clearing; teacher checks algebraic steps.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on linear_equations: multi-step and fraction equations.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one distributive-property equation and one fraction equation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with multi-step linear equations.

periods.objective: Identify equations with no solution or infinitely many solutions and apply to break-even problems.
periods.workedExamples: 9-1, Examples 4-5 — No Solution vs. Infinite Solutions; Real-World Break-Even Point
periods.notes: Special cases (no solution, infinite solutions) and real-world break-even modeling.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9b with one retrieval prompt on what happens when an equation simplifies to a false statement like 0 = 5.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-1, Examples 4-5 to develop: classify equations as having no solution (false statement), infinitely many solutions (true identity), or exactly one solution, and model a real-world break-even scenario by setting revenue equal to cost.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-1, Examples 4-5: classify solution types and set up break-even equations; teacher checks special-case identification.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on linear_equations: special-case classification and break-even problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one special-case identification and one break-even setup.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with special-case equations.

periods.objective: Graph linear equations using slope-intercept and standard form; write equations from two points.
periods.workedExamples: 9-2, Examples 1-3 — Graph from Slope-Intercept Form y = mx + b; Convert Standard Form to Slope-Intercept; Write Equation from Two Points
periods.notes: Core graphing from slope-intercept and standard form; equation writing from two points.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9c with one retrieval prompt identifying slope and y-intercept from the equation y = 3x − 2.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-2, Examples 1-3 to develop: graph a line directly from slope-intercept form by plotting the y-intercept and using the slope, convert standard form Ax + By = C to slope-intercept form, and write the equation of a line from two given points.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-2, Examples 1-3: graph lines, convert forms, and write equations from points; teacher checks slope calculation and graphing accuracy.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on graphing_linear_equations: graphing, form conversion, and equation writing from points.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one graph-from-slope-intercept and one write-equation-from-two-points problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with graphing linear equations.

periods.objective: Graph from point-slope form; identify parallel and perpendicular lines.
periods.workedExamples: 9-2, Examples 4-5 — Graph from Point-Slope Form; Parallel and Perpendicular Lines
periods.notes: Point-slope graphing and parallel/perpendicular relationships.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9d with one retrieval prompt on the point-slope form y − y₁ = m(x − x₁).
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-2, Examples 4-5 to develop: graph from point-slope form by identifying the point and slope, and identify parallel lines (equal slopes) and perpendicular lines (negative reciprocal slopes).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-2, Examples 4-5: graph from point-slope form and classify parallel/perpendicular; teacher checks slope-relationship reasoning.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on graphing_linear_equations: point-slope graphing and parallel/perpendicular identification.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one point-slope graphing problem and one parallel/perpendicular classification.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with point-slope form and line relationships.

periods.objective: Apply linear equations to real-world modeling.
periods.notes: Real-world distance-time modeling from two data points. Split from Period 4 to separate form-based graphing from contextual application and to give the real-world modeling example focused treatment.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9d with one retrieval prompt on how slope represents rate of change in a real-world context.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-2, Example 6 to develop: model a cyclist's distance from home over time using two data points, compute slope as speed, identify y-intercept as initial distance, and interpret the equation in context.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-2, Example 6: set up and interpret linear models from real-world scenarios; teacher checks contextual interpretation of slope and y-intercept.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on graphing_linear_equations: real-world linear modeling problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one real-world linear modeling problem requiring slope and y-intercept interpretation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with real-world linear modeling.

periods.objective: Solve one-variable linear inequalities and apply the sign-flip rule.
periods.workedExamples: 9-3, Examples 1-2 — Solve and Graph One-Variable Inequality; Flip Inequality When Multiplying/Dividing by Negative
periods.notes: One-variable inequality solving, number-line graphing, and the sign-flip rule.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9e with one retrieval prompt on the difference between solving an equation and solving an inequality.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-3, Examples 1-2 to develop: solve one-variable inequalities using the same steps as equations, graph solutions on a number line with open/closed circles, and apply the sign-flip rule when multiplying or dividing by a negative number.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-3, Examples 1-2: solve inequalities and graph on number lines; teacher checks sign-flip application.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on linear_inequalities: one-variable solving and number-line graphing.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one inequality problem requiring the sign-flip rule and one number-line graph.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with the sign-flip rule.

periods.objective: Solve compound inequalities (AND/OR) and graph solutions on a number line.
periods.notes: Compound AND/OR inequalities and number-line graphing of solution sets.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9f with one retrieval prompt distinguishing AND (intersection) from OR (union) in compound inequalities.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-3, Examples 3-4 to develop: solve AND compound inequalities (intersection of two solution sets) and represent on a number line, and solve OR compound inequalities (union of two solution sets) and graph the combined solution.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-3, Examples 3-4: solve and graph compound inequalities; teacher checks AND vs. OR identification.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on compound_inequalities: AND and OR solving with number-line graphing.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one AND compound inequality and one OR compound inequality problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with compound inequalities.

periods.objective: Graph linear inequalities in two variables and apply to real-world constraints.
periods.workedExamples: 9-3, Examples 5-6 — Graph Linear Inequality in Two Variables; Real-World Budget Constraint
periods.notes: Two-variable inequality graphing with solid/dashed boundaries and real-world constraint modeling. Split from Period 7 to separate compound one-variable inequalities from two-variable graphing and applications.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9f with one retrieval prompt on how to determine which side of a boundary line to shade.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-3, Examples 5-6 to develop: graph linear inequalities in two variables with solid boundary lines (≤, ≥) or dashed boundary lines (<, >), determine shading by testing a point, and model a real-world budget constraint with a two-variable inequality.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-3, Examples 5-6: graph two-variable inequalities and interpret constraint problems; teacher checks shading direction and boundary line selection.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on compound_inequalities: two-variable graphing and constraint problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one two-variable inequality graph and one constraint interpretation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with two-variable inequality graphing.

periods.notes: Mastery cycle on 9a-9f.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes due SRS items and submits one corrected-error reflection.

periods.notes: Mid-unit review across 9a-9f: equations, graphing, and inequalities.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems combining equation solving, graphing, and inequalities.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes review problem set and submits one self-identified area for improvement.

periods.notes: Mid-unit assessment on 9a-9f.

periods.objective: Solve systems of equations by graphing and substitution.
periods.workedExamples: 9-4, Examples 1-2 — Solve by Graphing; Solve by Substitution
periods.notes: System solving by graphing (finding intersection) and substitution (isolating and substituting).

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9g with one retrieval prompt listing two methods for solving systems of equations.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-4, Examples 1-2 to develop: solve a system by graphing both lines and finding the intersection point, and solve by substitution by isolating one variable and substituting into the other equation.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-4, Examples 1-2: apply graphing and substitution to systems; teacher checks method selection and algebraic accuracy.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on systems_of_equations: graphing and substitution problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one system solved by graphing and one solved by substitution.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with solving systems of equations.

periods.workedExamples: 9-4, Examples 3-4 — Solve by Elimination (Add); Solve by Elimination (Multiply First)
periods.notes: Elimination with and without multiplication to create opposite coefficients.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9h with one retrieval prompt on how adding two equations can eliminate a variable.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-4, Examples 3-4 to develop: solve a system by elimination when coefficients are already opposites (add directly), and solve by elimination after multiplying one or both equations to create opposite coefficients.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-4, Examples 3-4: apply elimination with and without multiplication; teacher checks coefficient setup and addition accuracy.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on systems_of_equations: elimination problems with varying complexity.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one elimination-by-addition and one elimination-with-multiplication problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with elimination.

periods.objective: Classify systems and apply systems to real-world mixture problems.
periods.workedExamples: 9-4, Examples 5-6 — No Solution and Infinitely Many Solutions; Real-World Mixture Problem
periods.notes: System classification (parallel/same-line) and mixture-problem modeling.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9i with one retrieval prompt on how to classify a system as consistent/inconsistent or dependent/independent.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-4, Examples 5-6 to develop: identify systems with no solution (parallel lines with equal slopes but different y-intercepts) and infinitely many solutions (same line), and solve a real-world mixture problem using a system of equations.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-4, Examples 5-6: classify systems and solve mixture problems; teacher checks classification reasoning.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on systems_of_equations: classification and mixture problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one system classification and one mixture problem setup.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with system classification.

periods.objective: Graph systems of linear inequalities and identify solution regions.
periods.workedExamples: 9-5, Examples 1-2 — Graph System of Two Linear Inequalities; Identify Solution Region
periods.notes: Introduction to systems of inequalities: graphing two linear inequalities and identifying overlapping solution regions. Split from Period 14 to separate system classification/mixture from inequality-system introduction.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9j with one retrieval prompt on how to graph a single linear inequality in two variables.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-5, Examples 1-2 to develop: graph a system of two linear inequalities on the same coordinate plane, shade each inequality's solution half-plane, and identify the overlapping solution region where both inequalities are satisfied.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-5, Examples 1-2: graph inequality systems and identify solution regions; teacher checks shading accuracy and boundary line type.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on systems_of_equations: graphing systems of inequalities and identifying solution regions.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one system of inequalities graph with solution region identified.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with graphing systems of inequalities.

periods.objective: Determine feasible regions and apply systems of inequalities to linear programming.
periods.workedExamples: 9-5, Examples 3-5 — System with No Solution; Determine if a Point Is in the Solution Region; Real-World Linear Programming Feasible Region
periods.notes: No-solution systems, point testing, corner points, and linear programming.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 9j with one retrieval prompt on how to test whether a point lies in the solution region of an inequality system.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 9-5, Examples 3-5 to develop: identify a system of inequalities with no solution, test whether a point satisfies all inequalities in a system, and find the feasible region and corner points for a real-world linear programming problem.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 9-5, Examples 3-5: graph systems, find feasible regions, and identify corner points; teacher checks corner-point identification.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on linear_programming: feasible region identification, point testing, and optimization problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one feasible region identification and one corner-point computation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with feasible regions and linear programming.

periods.notes: Mathematical practices focus: method selection for systems, graphical reasoning for inequalities, and error analysis.

nonInstructionArtifact.groupTask: Mathematical practices focus: method selection for systems, graphical reasoning for inequalities, and error analysis.
nonInstructionArtifact.sourceWorkedExamples: Use the prior instruction periods named in the notes as the problem bank.
nonInstructionArtifact.deliverable: Group solution poster or shared explanation with one strategy comparison.
nonInstructionArtifact.facilitationNotes: Assign roles for method selection, graphing, algebraic solving, and explanation.
nonInstructionArtifact.completionCriteria: Correct method, accurate result, clear justification, and response to peer question.

periods.notes: Mastery cycle on 9g-9j.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes due SRS items and submits one corrected-error reflection.

periods.notes: Unit 9 review across 9a-9j.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes review problem set and submits one self-identified area for improvement.

periods.notes: Unit 9 final review and targeted remediation across 9a-9j.

nonInstructionArtifact.objectiveScope: Unit 9 final review across 9a-9j with targeted remediation.
nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by mid-unit and end-of-unit evidence; target weakest objective clusters.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems combining systems, inequalities, and linear programming.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes final review problem set and submits reflection on growth areas.

periods.notes: Unit 9 assessment.
